one_line_takeaway: Sandlock enforces AI-agent process confinement (filesystem/network/IPC/syscalls) unprivileged via a Landlock-plus-seccomp split (kernel-enforced static rules, a narrow TOCTOU-safe supervisor for runtime decisions), adding only ~5ms startup and near-bare-metal Redis throughput versus Docker's ~249ms startup and ~74-77% throughput, while its pipeline operator lets prompt-injection-resistant stage decompositions (dual-LLM/CaMeL) be kernel-enforced rather than trusted to the LLM.
key_finding: All 10 deterministic effectiveness checks in Table 3 behaved as required (e.g., reads/writes outside granted scope denied, in-scope allowed; connect to non-allowlisted host refused, allowlisted host allowed; a stage without a private-data grant could not read it while a sibling stage with the grant could; fork past the process cap (-P 4) returned EAGAIN; allocation past a memory cap (-m 64M) was blocked/process terminated). Startup overhead: Sandlock adds ~5ms over bare metal for /bin/echo (Figure 4a gives 1.3ms bare metal vs 5.6ms Sandlock vs 249ms Docker -- roughly 44x faster end-to-end than Docker), attributed to Landlock ruleset construction and seccomp-fd setup. Redis 7 throughput (redis-benchmark, 100k requests, 50 concurrent clients, 256B values, median of 5 runs): SET 75.2k rps (Sandlock) vs 75.5k rps (bare metal, i.e. matching within noise) vs Docker at ~74% of bare-metal (Figure 4b shows Docker at 74%/77% of bare metal for SET/GET); GET 74.2k rps (Sandlock) vs 73.9k rps (bare metal). p99 latency: 0.51ms (Sandlock) vs 0.49ms (bare metal) vs 1.44ms (Docker, ~3x Sandlock/bare-metal). COW-fork: a 1,000-fork run took ~530ms (~530us/fork, ~1,900 forks/s), dominated by the ptrace creation handshake needed for policy_fn-safe child registration; disabling policy_fn increased fork rate by another ~30%. Network on-behalf path cost: for a 256B request/response echo with both endpoints sandboxed, median round-trip rose from 20us (bare metal) to 55us (Sandlock, ~35us added; p99 33us vs 114us), while Docker (established connections stay in-kernel) matched bare metal at 19us median -- the paper argues this fixed per-message cost is immaterial against real agent network workloads (LLM API calls at 100ms-10s). Compatibility: python3, make, node, and pytest ran unmodified once given their scratch/temp directories (e.g. /tmp), including Python native-extension imports; a systematic compatibility study is left as future work.
core_contribution: Sandlock, an unprivileged Linux process sandbox for confining AI-agent-executed code (shell commands, third-party scripts, MCP tool invocations) on developer workstations. Its design contribution is a split enforcement architecture: static, input-independent policy (filesystem read/write scope, TCP-port scope, IPC boundaries, unconditional syscall denials) is compiled into kernel-enforced Landlock and seccomp-bpf rules with no supervisor involvement, while runtime-dependent decisions (resolved connect() destinations, execve argv, HTTP method/host/path, filesystem writes that must be captured rather than applied directly) are routed through a narrow seccomp user-notification supervisor. On top of this split the paper builds: (1) a TOCTOU-safe programmable policy hook (policy_fn) that exposes syscall events including execve argv to host code without violating kernel re-read semantics, by freezing all processes/threads that could alias the child's address space (via /proc enumeration plus ptrace seize/interrupt, and ptrace-traced clone/fork/vfork/clone3) before reading pointer arguments, and by never Continuing the original syscall (it is always answered with Allow/Deny/on-behalf substitution); (2) an unprivileged copy-on-write workspace (seccomp-mediated redirect-on-write, plus an alternative dedicated COW filesystem backend, BranchFS) supporting commit/abort/keep semantics and a --dry-run mode, requiring no mount namespaces or root; (3) a COW-fork primitive that forks N confined workers sharing copy-on-write initialized pages for map-reduce-style agent workloads; (4) a pipeline operator (|) that composes multiple independently confined process stages connected by kernel pipes, so that capability separation assumed by dual-LLM/CaMeL-style prompt-injection defenses (the stage touching untrusted content has no network; the stage with network never sees untrusted data) is kernel-enforced rather than left to LLM judgment; and (5) an HTTP/HTTPS access-control layer that redirects intercepted HTTP flows to a local proxy enforcing method/host/path rules (opt-in TLS interception requires installing a sandbox CA; otherwise HTTPS is governed only by endpoint allowlisting). The system is implemented in ~7K lines of Rust with a CLI frontend and Python/Rust FFI, and requires Landlock ABI 6+ (Linux 6.12+); it is presented as a workshop paper (Agentic OS Workshop, March 2026) with an explicitly 'preliminary' evaluation.
threat_model: The sandboxed process may execute attacker-influenced code or consume attacker-controlled data (malicious/compromised package scripts, downloaded files, tool outputs, LLM-generated commands). Trusted: the host kernel, the Sandlock supervisor, and the embedding agent runtime. Out of scope: kernel vulnerabilities, side channels, privileged local attackers, and deliberate exhaustion of shared kernel resources (the paper explicitly notes cgroups remain the stronger defense against adversarial kernel-memory exhaustion, since Sandlock's own resource caps are cooperative/syscall-interception-based, not kernel-enforced allocation limits). The paper frames five workload properties that shape the design: untrusted code is short-lived/frequent (so per-invocation startup latency matters); the host is a developer machine (so confinement must be unprivileged and default-deny outside a workspace); network access is necessary but dangerous, motivating destination- and HTTP-level allowlisting rather than just IP/port; agent tasks compose stages of asymmetric trust, explicitly invoked via Simon Willison's 'lethal trifecta' (private data + untrusted content + external communication in one context) and the dual-LLM/CaMeL decomposition pattern, which the pipeline operator is built to enforce at the kernel level; and agent behavior/policy needs are only knowable at runtime (e.g., an npm install phase needing network access transitioning to a test-run phase that should not retain it), motivating the policy_fn runtime hook. The paper is explicit that argv/binary-name matching in policy_fn is 'an observation and policy-tightening signal, not a containment boundary' since it is evadable by renaming/reimplementing tools -- actual containment comes only from the kernel-enforced Landlock/seccomp/network-ACL layers.
limitations: The paper self-labels the evaluation 'preliminary': a single workstation/kernel configuration, no adversarial/fuzzing evaluation of the seccomp filter or TOCTOU defenses, and effectiveness checks (Table 3) are 10 hand-picked deterministic cases run once each rather than a systematic security test suite. Resource limits (memory, process count, CPU) are enforced cooperatively via syscall interception rather than kernel-enforced at allocation time; the memory bound covers only address space requested through mmap/mremap/brk/shmget (not resident pages), main-thread stack growth via RLIMIT_STACK is not counted, and the paper states plainly that for adversarial workloads aiming to exhaust kernel memory through corner-case syscalls, cgroups remain stronger. TOCTOU-safety for policy_fn depends on successfully freezing every process/thread that could alias the child's address space via ptrace seize/interrupt and clone-event tracing; if ptrace is unavailable (e.g., due to Yama ptrace restrictions), the paper states the syscall is denied with EPERM rather than silently relaxed -- but this means policy_fn functionality itself becomes unavailable in some hardened/nested-sandbox environments. policy_fn argv/binary-name inspection is explicitly acknowledged by the authors as evadable (an adversary can rename or reimplement a tool) and is described only as an audit/tightening signal, not a containment boundary -- real containment comes solely from the static Landlock/seccomp/network-ACL layers. The pipeline operator enforces capability separation between stages but the paper states two explicit caveats: it bounds stage capabilities, not pipe content (a compromised upstream stage can still shape bytes consumed downstream, so data-provenance defenses remain necessary), and Sandlock does not prove that a decomposition is sound (i.e., that the untrusted-content stage truly never needs network) -- that responsibility remains with the policy author. Effect rollback covers filesystem writes only; network side effects (e.g., an HTTP request already sent) are not currently captured/rollback-able, which the paper lists as future work. Requires a fairly recent kernel (Landlock ABI 6+, i.e. Linux 6.12+) and refuses to run below that floor rather than downgrading, which the paper frames as a safety choice but which limits deployability on older systems. Kernel vulnerabilities, side channels, privileged local attackers, and shared-kernel-resource exhaustion are explicitly out of scope of the threat model; for multi-tenant adversarial workloads the paper itself recommends a microVM instead.

quantitative_results: Startup latency (/bin/echo, median of 20 runs): bare metal 1.3ms, Sandlock 5.6ms (~5ms overhead), Docker 249ms (~44x slower than Sandlock)
Redis SET throughput (redis-benchmark, 100k requests, 50 concurrent clients, 256B values, median of 5 runs): bare metal 75.5k rps, Sandlock 75.2k rps (within noise), Docker ~74% of bare-metal
Redis GET throughput: bare metal 73.9k rps, Sandlock 74.2k rps (within noise), Docker ~77% of bare-metal
Redis p99 latency: bare metal 0.49ms, Sandlock 0.51ms, Docker 1.44ms (~3x Sandlock)

Effectiveness micro-benchmark (Table 3): 10/10 deterministic deny/allow policy checks behaved as required (out-of-scope read/write denied, in-scope allowed; non-allowlisted connect refused, allowlisted allowed; cross-stage private-data isolation held; fork-past-cap returned EAGAIN; alloc-past-memory-cap blocked)
COW-fork throughput: ~530ms for 1,000 forks (~530us/fork, ~1,900 forks/s); disabling policy_fn increases fork rate by ~30%

Network on-behalf overhead (256B echo round trip, both endpoints sandboxed): bare metal 20us median, Sandlock 55us median (~35us added, p99 33us vs 114us), Docker 19us median (matches bare metal since established connections stay in-kernel)
baselines_compared: Docker (rootful, for performance benchmarks; rootless, for the Table 1 feature comparison)
